region_of_origin: Thailand / Singapore (Thai-style seafood noodle dish served in Singapore)
food_subgroup: Seafood noodle dish — rice vermicelli with fish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Atlantic snow codfish vermicelli. FOODON_00002572 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products — cooked fish dish with noodles (closest parent)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Atlantic snow codfish vermicelli. A0CKP (fish-based composite dish) is the closest defensible parent in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific composite seafood noodle dish.

main_ingredients: Atlantic snow codfish (Gadus morhua or Eleginus gracilis)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Atlantic Snow Codfish Vermicelli weighs approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of poached or simmered snow cod fillet, a serving of rice vermicelli noodles, and a clear or mildly seasoned fish broth with aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Atlantic cod (cooked, dry heat) + rice vermicelli (cooked) + fish broth composite
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Atlantic Snow Codfish Vermicelli exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of: (1) USDA FDC #171956 Atlantic cod, cooked, dry heat (~35% by weight of dish); (2) USDA FDC #169730 Rice noodles, cooked (~40% by weight); (3) fish broth/stock (~25% by weight, USDA FDC #172234). Proportions are based on typical Thai-style seafood vermicelli dish construction. Values represent a plausible per-100g estimate for the assembled dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles / vermicelli analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Atlantic Snow Codfish Vermicelli. GI is estimated from the primary glycemic carbohydrate contributor: rice vermicelli (bee hoon). The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021) report GI values for rice noodles/vermicelli in the range of 49–58 (mean ~53, low GI category). The fish and broth components contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. GI of 53 is adopted as the best defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 47.25) / 100 ≈ 25.0, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 10.5 × 4.5 = 47.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish broth and soy sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 450 g serving provides approximately 1395 mg sodium, which represents 58–70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2400 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sodium broth and limit soy sauce additions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 53 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 25 per standard serving, this dish has a moderate impact on postprandial blood glucose; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of the vermicelli component and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to reduce the overall glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein with low saturated fat and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Atlantic cod is a lean white fish with low saturated fat (~0.2 g per 100 g) and provides omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids; regular consumption of lean fish is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk, though the sodium content from broth and seasoning should be moderated in patients with heart failure or hypertensive cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Atlantic Snow Codfish Vermicelli is a Thai-influenced seafood noodle dish served in Singapore, featuring poached or gently simmered Atlantic snow cod fillets served over or alongside thin rice vermicelli noodles (bee hoon) in a clear, lightly seasoned fish broth. The dish is prepared by simmering the cod in a stock flavoured with ginger, garlic, spring onion, light soy sauce, and white pepper, then ladling the broth over pre-soaked or briefly boiled rice vermicelli. The result is a delicate, mildly savoury bowl with a clean fish flavour, soft noodle texture, and a light, aromatic broth. Atlantic snow cod — a term used in Singapore seafood markets to describe premium white-fleshed cod species — is prized for its firm yet flaky texture, mild sweetness, and low fat content. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-calorie, high-protein, low-fat meal providing approximately 82 kcal per 100 g, with 7.8 g protein and 10.5 g carbohydrate. It is a good source of vitamin B12, selenium, niacin, and phosphorus from the fish component. The primary clinical consideration is sodium, as the broth and soy sauce seasoning can contribute significant sodium per serving. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low), driven by the rice vermicelli component. This dish is suitable for individuals seeking a lean, high-protein seafood meal and is commonly ordered at Thai seafood restaurants in Singapore such as Shrimp Prawn Seafood at Boat Quay.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Atlantic Snow Codfish Vermicelli was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or ICMR-NIN databases. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FDC entries for Atlantic cod (cooked), rice noodles (cooked), and fish broth. GI is estimated from the analogous rice vermicelli GI value from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible but not based on a direct measurement of this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for Atlantic cod (cooked, dry heat; FDC #171956), rice noodles cooked (FDC #169730), and fish broth (FDC #172234), weighted at approximately 35%, 40%, and 25% respectively. These proportions reflect a typical Thai-style seafood vermicelli bowl construction. 2. The term 'Atlantic Snow Codfish' is a Singapore seafood market trade name; it may refer to Atlantic cod (Gadus morhua) or related cold-water white fish species. Nutrient values for Atlantic cod are used as the primary fish reference. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as Atlantic cod and rice noodles contain no industrially produced trans fats per USDA FDC data — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. Sodium content (310 mg per 100 g) reflects a moderately seasoned broth; actual sodium may vary significantly depending on restaurant preparation and soy sauce quantity. 5. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care). 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all micronutrients are estimated from USDA FDC composite data and carry medium confidence. 7. The dish is served at Shrimp Prawn Seafood - Boat Quay, Singapore, at SGD 50.80, which is consistent with a premium seafood restaurant price point for Atlantic cod. 8. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as the closest parent for a fish-based composite dish with noodles; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific dish. 9. FOODON_00002572 is used as the closest parent noodle dish category; no exact FoodOn term exists for Atlantic snow codfish vermicelli. 10. Hindi local name in aliases_by_language is अटलांटिक स्नो कॉड मछली सेवई (Devanagari script); this exact string appears verbatim in the flat aliases list.
